title: Data centres not tied to copyright overhaul
full_text:
Jim Chalmers has rejected arguments from AI developers that Australia must overhaul its copyright protections to enable tens of billions of dollars’ worth of investment to flow into data centre infrastructure.
The technology sector argues the copyright regime weakens the nation’s attractiveness as an investment destination, as AI firms are barred from scraping local creative works, data and other protected material to train models.
Speaking in Canberra on Wednesday, the Treasurer dismissed the industry’s case, saying booming growth in spending on data centres and other AI-related investment was not predicated on altering the copyright regime.
“We’ve made our position clear when it comes to copyright, and that hasn’t been a barrier to this quite extraordinary and very welcome investment that we’ve seen in that part of our economy,” he told reporters.
Capital expenditure figures released late last month showed the strongest quarterly growth in investment in machinery and equipment in more than three decades, with the surge driven by a 196 per cent increase in information and technology-related spending.
The build-out in AI-linked infrastructure has become one of the largest globally. Australia ranks sixth for data centres that are operational, under construction or in the pipeline – behind the US, China, Malaysia, India and Japan.
Westpac estimates the nation’s data centre investment pipeline exceeds $155bn once associated energy generation and transmission projects are included, as the scale of spending raises concerns about potential strain on the grid and pressure on water supplies.
With tech giants advocating for copyright reform, the Albanese government is weighing potential changes to copyright law but repeatedly has ruled out issuing a blanket text and data mining exemption for AI training, despite calls from developers.
During Senate question time on Tuesday, independent ACT senator David Pocock pressed the government on its proposed reforms, claiming he had received a confidential tip-off that it was considering a copyright “carve-out” for AI companies.
The proposal would be contingent on billions of dollars’ worth of investment in data centre infrastructure and contributions to a new fund supporting the creative industries, he asserted. An alternative proposal, he claimed, would expand existing licensing arrangements to allow AI developers to access Australian content. Both mooted options were immediately dismissed as “reckless speculation” by Labor.
